docs.title: Tuesday, July 21, 2026
docs.text: Tuesday, July 21, 2026 Cold Open Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. It's Tuesday, July 21. I'm Sam. And I'm Alex. Here's the thread we're pulling this morning: think of today as referendum day. For a week the story has been an AI-stock panic — a chip rout that spread from one name to the whole complex and across to Asia. And then, quietly, on Monday, it stopped. It didn't just stop, it reversed. Semis were the green on the board yesterday, and this morning the futures are pointing higher — Nasdaq-100 up more than 1%. The thing that was breaking is, for now, un-breaking. And the timing is almost too neat, because after the close tonight the first Magnificent-7 verdicts land — Tesla, Alphabet, and Intel all report. The very names at the center of the wobble finally get to answer with numbers instead of sentiment. So the panic pauses right as the jury gets seated. But — and this is the whole point — the pressure didn't vanish. It moved. It migrated. Out of equities and into the oil-and-rates vise. Brent is toggling around 90 dollars on whether a 10-day Iran ceasefire actually holds, the 10-year yield has climbed to 4.60%, and the Fed meets a week from today with a live hold-versus-hike debate. So the mic passes from sentiment to fundamentals. Let's get into it. Two world stories first. Headlines Start with the one bleeding into everything else — Hormuz. And for the first time in weeks, the oil tape actually went two ways. That's the tell. Brent poked above 91 dollars intraday Monday, and then faded back toward 88, because there are suddenly signs of renewed US-Iran diplomatic contact — Qatari mediation, and reporting of a proposed 10-day ceasefire. Which is a genuine shift in what the market is pricing. For two weeks crude has only traded escalation. Monday was the first session where it traded de-escalation odds — the war premium moving in the tails, not just spot. And yet the physical picture is still ugly. A products tanker, the Kavomaleas, halted and anchored empty near Musandam after Iran's navy targeted vessels. That's 9 ships attacked since July 6. War-risk insurance is running roughly 8 times pre-crisis. And the strait is effectively closed — you had something like 10 transits on July 12 against a normal 88. So even as the diplomatic headline pulls the price down, the plumbing says the risk is very much still on. So the trade now is a coin held mid-air. If the 10-day ceasefire is real, you get a fast unwind of the premium. If it collapses again — and it has collapsed before — you're straight back above 90 with a closed strait. Right. Which is exactly why the second story matters, because there's now a second front pointed at the oil complex — and it's Ukraine. This was the deadliest set of strikes inside Russia in more than two years. Over July 19 and 20, Ukraine destroyed 7 Russian "shadow fleet" vessels — the tankers Moscow uses to dodge the oil sanctions — plus power substations in Crimea. That's on top of 13 substations and 4 vessels the day before. And Russia answered in kind — roughly 40 Iskander-M and Zircon missiles on Kyiv, and a drone strike that started a major fire in Sumy. So this is a real escalation, not a probe. But the piece that ties back to our throughline is the target selection. Ukraine is now systematically hitting the sanctions-evasion export fleet — the ships that actually move Russian barrels. So you've got two separate wars both squeezing the same barrel — Iran throttling the strait, Ukraine going after Russia's ability to sell around sanctions. The oil premium isn't a one-country story anymore. It's structural. And that's the fuse under everything the Fed does next week. And it changes how you read a ceasefire, doesn't it? Even if Iran and the US settle, the Ukraine-Russia front keeps a bid under crude. That's the subtle point. A month ago, "ceasefire" would have meant the premium drains out cleanly. Now there are two hands on the tap, and only one of them is at the negotiating table. So the floor under oil is higher than the headlines suggest — which is precisely the Fed's problem. Which is the perfect handoff to the tape. Let's do the markets. Market Overview So here's Monday's close, and the headline is: mild risk-off, but not the risk-off you'd expect from the week we just had. Give me the board. S&P 500 finished at 7,443.28, down 14.41 points, off 0.19%. The Nasdaq Composite was basically flat — 25,508.07, down just 12 points, a rounding error. The Dow was the laggard, down 307 points to 51,839.26, off 0.59%. And the Russell 2000 was the worst of the four, down 0.67% to 2,942.43. And that shape is the whole story. Because if this were still the AI panic, the Nasdaq is the one bleeding and the Dow holds up. This is the opposite. Exactly the opposite. Semis actually led green. Broadcom up 1.98%, Intel up 2.13%, Micron up 1.94%, AMD up 1.58%, Taiwan Semi up 1%, Nvidia flat but green. The chip rout didn't just pause — it bounced. So what pulled the tape down, if not chips? Single names and old-economy weight. Apple was down 2.14%, and because it's a price-weighted drag it took a chunk out of the Dow single-handed. Oracle was down 4%. Dell down 3.69%. That's what a Dow-and-small-cap down day looks like — it's not a growth-stock unwind. And the bond market is where the actual pressure showed up. Yields up across the curve. The 2-year at 4.21%, up 3 basis points. The 10-year at 4.60%, up 5 — it was 4.55 on Friday. The 30-year at 5.11%. That is the vise tightening, and it's oil-driven, not growth-driven. And the tell that it's oil and not fear — gold went up with yields. Gold at 4,068.90, up 1.32%. When gold and yields rise together, that's a reflation and war-premium signal, not a flight to safety. A fear bid buys bonds. This sold them. Currencies? Dollar-yen at 162.70, the yen still pinned near record-weak — the carry trade lives. Euro at 1.1420. And crude we covered — Brent 90.34 at the settle, up 1.26%. And then the overnight flips the mood again. It does. This morning US futures are higher — Nasdaq-100 up about 1.3%, S&P up half a percent, Dow up a bit. Chips reviving again, and Brent easing toward 89.80 on that ceasefire push. So the machine keeps toggling. One data point worth flagging — Germany. Yes. German ZEW economic sentiment blew out — 26.3 against an expected 18, up from 10.5. The EU-wide read was 23.4. That's a real optimism surprise out of Europe, and it lands right as European earnings are coming in strong. Roughly 87% of the S&P names that have reported this season have beaten on EPS. So put the two halves together — a rebounding chip complex, a beat-rate near 87%, and a European confidence spike — and the "global risk-off" narrative starts to look overdone. It does. What actually happened this week is a positioning unwind dressed up as a fundamental scare. The bodies were crowded into the same momentum names, and when they de-grossed, it looked like the sky was falling. Underneath, the earnings kept printing. That's the gap tonight's numbers get to close. Which brings us to the main event tonight. U.S. Business After the close tonight, the earnings jury gets seated. Tesla, Alphabet, and Intel all report Q2 — the first Magnificent-7 prints of the season. And this is the referendum. For a week the AI trade has been sold on sentiment and a Gemini-delay scare. Tonight the actual numbers arrive. The consensus bars: Alphabet is seen at 2.90 dollars of EPS, up 25.5%, on 116.9 billion dollars of revenue, up 21.3%. Tesla at 54 cents, up 35%, on 26.4 billion in revenue. And 73 companies report today all told. The Alphabet number is the one I'd watch, because the whole "AI leaders get punished for any deceleration" theme lives or dies on the cloud growth rate and the capex guide. Right — it's not the headline beat, it's the second derivative. Does cloud reaccelerate, and does the capex number go up again? If it does, the buildout story we talk about every day gets a fresh receipt. If the guide wobbles, the de-rating has more room. And Tesla is almost a different company to think about — margins, the energy-storage business, and whatever they say about autonomy. Fair caveat for everyone listening: all of this lands after the bell, so at this hour it's the setup, not the result. But the setup is the market's entire focus today. Second US story, and it's a cleaner read on the real economy — Charles Schwab. Schwab reports today, and the preview is a machine running hot. Consensus around 1.55 dollars of EPS against 1.14 a year ago — up roughly 37% — on about 6.9 billion in revenue, up 18%. The asset-gathering piece is the tell for me. It's remarkable — record total client assets of 13.14 trillion dollars, up 27% year on year, and a best-ever May for net new assets at 49.9 billion. That's the retail-brokerage read on the "resilient consumer plus higher-for-longer rates" world — they earn more on cash, and the client money keeps coming in. And underneath the AI drama, the boring earnings breadth held up too. It did. GM beat and raised its full-year profit guide — stock up a couple percent. 3M beat and popped around 7%. This is the point we keep making: there's a whole non-AI economy compounding quietly, and it's what makes an AI de-rating survivable rather than systemic. And that's the frame for tonight, really. If Tesla and Alphabet disappoint, the question isn't whether the market can absorb it — the breadth says it can. The question is whether the multiple can. Exactly right. The earnings have been fine. What's fragile is the price you're paying for the growth. A miss doesn't break the economy; it re-rates the premium. And that's a much more contained problem than the tape has been acting like all week. Let's take that breadth international. International Business & Politics Two from overseas, and the first is the European version of that same "earnings without an AI story" trade — Novartis. They reported this morning, and it was a beat. Core operating income of 5.94 billion dollars against roughly 5.31 billion expected, and they reaffirmed full-year guidance — low-single-digit sales growth. The momentum is coming off the launch portfolio: Kisqali, Kesimpta, Scemblix, Pluvicto — plus pipeline progress, including a del-zota FDA submission. This is exactly the trans-Atlantic split we've flagged. European large-caps are printing genuinely strong earnings, but because there's no AI narrative attached, the market pays them a value multiple, not a growth multiple. Right — a Swiss pharma beating on drug launches while it manages a patent cliff is a beautiful business. It just doesn't get the same re-rating a US hyperscaler gets for the same growth. That gap is the opportunity, or the trap, depending on your view. Second international story is Brussels flexing — and it's aimed at China. The European Commission hit AliExpress — the Alibaba-owned marketplace — with a record 550-million-euro fine under the Digital Services Act. That's for failing to curb illegal, counterfeit, and unsafe goods on the platform. And the size is the message. 550 million euros dwarfs the 200 million Temu fine and the 120 million X fine. It does. AliExpress has an action plan due October 20, and it's calling the fine "disproportionate." But the read-through is bigger than one company — Brussels is turning the DSA into a genuine cost center for Chinese cross-border commerce. It's trade friction with a price tag, wearing a consumer-protection jacket. Which sets up the one China story nicely. China The policy-response shoe finally dropped in Beijing. After second-quarter growth slipped below the official target range, the State Council held an executive meeting chaired by Premier Li Qiang. And they blinked toward stimulus. They pledged to hit the roughly 5% full-year growth goal and — the phrase from the Xinhua readout — to "maximize efficient use of fiscal funds." Markets read that as accelerated infrastructure and investment spending in the second half. This is the follow-on to the GDP undershoot we covered, not a recap of it. The number was the diagnosis; this is the treatment. And it's the same bind China's been in all year. They can lean on fiscal spending to hit the number, but the underlying problem is overcapacity and weak domestic demand — which keeps them export-reliant, which is exactly what's drawing fines like the AliExpress one and tariffs elsewhere. It's a two-speed economy trying to spend its way to the target. And the deflation angle is the global piece — a China exporting overcapacity is a China exporting disinflation, which normally is a gift to the Fed. Except right now the Fed's problem is oil, not goods. So the usual China deflation anchor is getting overwhelmed by the war premium. That's the world in one sentence today. Let's go to the desk that lives one layer under all of this — energy. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities And we're sourcing this one from the practitioner newsletters this morning — the CTVC and Utility Dive editions — so let's go deep. What's the lead? The lead is a groundbreaking that tells you where hyperscaler power procurement is actually heading. Cypress Creek broke ground July 14 on the Steel River Energy Center in Mississippi County, Arkansas. Scale it for me. Phases 1 and 2 are 1.6 gigawatts DC of solar plus 1.9 gigawatt-hours of battery storage. The full three-phase build is 2.5 gigawatts DC and 2.9 gigawatt-hours by 2029. And here's the tell — Google has a power-purchase agreement with Cypress Creek covering phases 1 and 2. It's Google's largest solar-plus-storage offtake globally. And there's a second package alongside it. There is — a separate Entergy Arkansas deal, Cypress Solar, 600 megawatts of PV and 350 megawatt-hours of storage near Pine Bluff, and that one underpins a 4-billion-dollar Google data center build. So what's the second-order read? Because on the surface this just looks like a big renewables deal. The read is that hyperscalers are now anchoring merchant-scale solar-plus-storage directly — bypassing the utility's integrated resource plan entirely — and siting it on cheap industrial land in a steel county. This isn't ESG box-ticking. Solar is being bought as a firming input for a data center. And watch the storage ratio — 2.9 gigawatt-hours is sized to shift power across hours, not just shave a peak. Which connects to the cost story, because gas isn't the cheap answer people assume anymore. This is the Lazard 2026 number and it's striking. Unsubsidized, utility-scale solar is at 40 dollars a megawatt-hour, onshore wind at 37, against a combined-cycle gas floor of 51. Everything is rising on capex and rates — but new-build gas is now at a 15-year-high levelized cost. And yet everyone's ordering gas anyway. That's the paradox. Announced gas capacity is surging on demand-forecast revisions and long equipment lead times — even as gas is the most expensive it's been in 15 years to build. So developers are paying up for dispatchability and speed, not economics. The clean read: solar-plus-storage is the near-term megawatt, and gas is the firm tail you pay a premium for. Give me the policy piece, because FERC moved again. FERC's July open meeting — and this is distinct from the large-load show-cause we covered. This time FERC ordered NERC to file initial reliability standards for large computational loads — data centers and crypto — plus load-registry criteria, by year-end. So this is reliability, not cost allocation. Exactly the distinction. Everything to date has been about who pays. This is about how these loads have to behave on the grid — registration, ride-through, the ability to stay online or shed cleanly during a disturbance. It's a whole new compliance surface for data-center developers. FERC also told CAISO and SPP to file a joint seams-coordination report by September 30, which points at West-wide interregional transfer as a relief valve for load growth. And there's a cautionary tale in here too — the New York cable. The Champlain Hudson Power Express. 1,250 megawatts, 6 billion dollars, a 339-mile high-voltage DC line. It energized July 1 and then went dark repeatedly on a terrestrial cable fault — likely offline for most of July, during a heat wave. Governor Hochul called it "unacceptable." And the lesson for the data-center world is the redundancy one. It's the N-minus-1 lesson. New York bet roughly 1.25 gigawatts of firm import on a single new HVDC tie with no redundancy, and one fault took it all out. Every data-center campus leaning on one big interconnection should read that as a warning — and it reinforces the behind-the-meter, on-site generation case we keep coming back to. If your whole load hangs on one wire, you don't have firm power. You have a single point of failure. And it ties the whole segment together, actually. The Google-Arkansas deal, the gas paradox, the FERC reliability rule — they're all circling the same anxiety. They are. The anxiety is deliverability. Everyone can name a gigawatt of nameplate capacity somewhere; the scarce thing is a firm electron at a specific substation on a specific date. That's why Google is co-locating solar-plus-storage on-site, why developers pay a 15-year-high price for gas turbines they can actually get, and why FERC suddenly cares whether these loads can ride through a disturbance. So the buildout isn't constrained by capital anymore. It's constrained by delivery. Capital is the easy part. The binding constraints are turbines, transformers, interconnection slots, and now regulatory ride-through. And the CHPE outage is the whole thesis in one cable — you can spend 6 billion dollars and still not have firm power if there's no redundancy behind it. Well said. Let's go to Manila. Philippines Weather first, and the good news is there's no named storm — but there's a system worth watching. What's PAGASA saying? No active typhoon, no signals up. The habagat — the southwest monsoon — is bringing isolated rains and thunderstorms across Metro Manila, Ilocos, the Cordillera, MIMAROPA, Western Visayas, and the Zambales-Bataan-Cavite-Batangas corridor. But the new wrinkle: cloud clusters east of Visayas and Mindanao consolidated into a low-pressure area as of 8pm Monday, with a medium chance of becoming a tropical depression within 24 hours. So the last system, Inday-Bavi, is gone, and this is the next one forming. Right — fresh system, still unnamed, still just an LPA. Watching it, not sounding an alarm. And the peso is the macro story, because it's flirting with the record again. It closed at 61.745 to the dollar on Monday — Friday it was 61.686. And the all-time low is 61.75. So it's within a fraction of a centavo of the record. What's driving it? RCBC's Michael Ricafort ties it straight to the oil story — dollar strength as crude sits near one-month highs for a second straight session, Hormuz traffic near-stalled, and that proposed 10-day ceasefire keeping everyone guessing. This is our throughline landing in a specific currency: the Philippines imports almost all its oil, so a war premium is an imported inflation tax and a peso problem at the same time. And the central bank did something interesting — a verbal move. This is the new angle. The BSP warned banks against using FX derivatives to profit off the peso's volatility, and Malacañang reaffirmed the BSP as the lead FX-stabilizing institution. So without touching the policy rate, the BSP is trying to curb the speculative feedback loop — jawboning the banks to keep the currency slide orderly. Which is a very different tool than a hike. It is — and it tells you they'd rather not spend reserves or hike into an oil shock if a firm word can slow the momentum. It's defense by other means. And there's the politics too — the SONA is coming. Marcos delivers his fifth State of the Nation Address on July 27. The police are finalizing security — deploying 20,229 personnel, plus military and coast guard reinforcements, around the Batasang Pambansa. At least four groups have applied for rally permits in Quezon City. And the Palace is framing this one as "simple but dignified" — no celebrity directors, no A-list performers for what's his penultimate SONA. A more austere tone for a president heading into the back half of his term. And a telling one — you go austere on the pageantry when the household budget is the story. Which it is. Between a near-record peso, an imported oil bill, and core inflation that's been hardening, the SONA lands into a cost-of-living moment. So "simple but dignified" is as much fiscal signal as it is stagecraft. Alright — that's the news. Time to switch languages. Vocab Of The Day 今天我们学四个词，两个中文，两个他加禄语，都跟今天的新闻有关系。(Today we learn four words — two Mandarin, two Tagalog, all tied to today's news.) 第一个词：究竟。(The first word: jiūjìng.) 究竟。我们今天一直在问一个问题：市场究竟在担心什么？是芯片，还是石油和利率？(Jiūjìng. We've been asking one question all day: what exactly is the market worried about — chips, or oil and rates?) 对，"究竟" 就是用来加强这个问题的语气 —— 到底、真正是什么。它是一个书面语，比较正式。(Right — jiūjìng intensifies the question: what is it really, after all. It's written-language, fairly formal.) 那它跟 "到底" 有什么区别？(So how is it different from dàodǐ?) 意思差不多，但是 "到底" 更口语，"究竟" 更书面。你在财经评论里常看到 "究竟"，在聊天里常用 "到底"。(The meaning is close, but dàodǐ is more spoken and jiūjìng more written. You see jiūjìng in financial commentary, dàodǐ in casual chat.) 明白。所以我可以说：投资者究竟该不该相信这次反弹？(Got it. So I can say: should investors really trust this rebound, after all?) 完美 —— 而且你刚才用了第二个词：反弹。(Perfect — and you just used the second word: fǎntán.) 反弹！这个词今天太合适了。星期一芯片股大幅反弹，绿了一整天。(Fǎntán! So fitting today. Monday chip stocks rebounded sharply, green all day.) 对。"反弹" 是从低点弹回来，通常很快、很猛。金融里常说 "技术性反弹" 或者 "报复性反弹"。(Right. Fǎntán is bouncing back off a low — usually fast and sharp. In finance you say "technical rebound" or "revenge rebound.") 那 "反弹" 和 "回升" 呢？(And fǎntán versus huíshēng?) 好问题。"回升" 是慢慢地、稳稳地涨回去；"反弹" 是突然地弹一下。所以市场情绪是 "反弹"，经济数据是 "回升"。(Good question. Huíshēng is a slow, steady climb back; fǎntán is a sudden bounce. So sentiment "rebounds," economic data "recovers.") 所以今天的标题应该是：芯片股反弹，但投资者究竟会不会追？(So today's headline is: chips rebound, but will investors really chase it?) 说得好。现在换他加禄语。第一个词：bumawi。(Well put. Now to Tagalog. The first word: bumawi.) Bumawi. Ang ibig sabihin, mabawi ang natalo o nawala — tulad ng merkado na bumawi ng lugi noong Lunes. (Bumawi means to recoup what was lost — like the market recouping its losses on Monday.) Tama. Ang ugat ay "bawi." Kapag sinabi mong "bumawi ng lugi," ibig sabihin binalik mo ang perang nawala. (Right. The root is bawi. When you say bumawi ng lugi, you mean you won back the money you lost.) Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "bumawi" at "bumangon"? (What's the difference between bumawi and bumangon?) Magandang tanong. Ang "bumangon" ay tumayong muli pagkatapos madapa — mas malawak, mas pisikal. Ang "bumawi" ay partikular: binabawi mo ang isang bagay na nawala sa iyo. (Good question. Bumangon is to get back on your feet after falling — broader, more physical. Bumawi is specific: you're winning back something that was taken from you.) Kaya sa merkado: bumawi ang mga semiconductor, pero hindi pa bumabangon nang lubusan ang buong sektor. (So in the market: the semiconductors recouped, but the whole sector hasn't fully gotten back on its feet yet.) Eksakto. At ang huling salita: pigilin. (Exactly. And the last word: pigilin.) Pigilin. Ang ugat ay "pigil" — ibig sabihin, hadlangan ang isang bagay na kumikilos na. (Pigilin — the root is pigil, meaning to hold back something already in motion.) Tulad ng BSP na sinusubukang pigilin ang pagbagsak ng piso — binabalaan ang mga bangko laban sa FX derivatives. (Like the BSP trying to curb the peso's slide — warning the banks against FX derivatives.) Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "pigilin" at "hadlangan"? (What's the difference between pigilin and hadlangan?) Ang "hadlangan" ay harangan nang tuluyan — parang pader. Ang "pigilin" ay pabagalin o supilin ang isang puwersang gumagalaw na — hindi mo tuluyang mapapatigil, pero mapapabagal mo. (Hadlangan is to block outright — like a wall. Pigilin is to slow or restrain a force already moving — you may not stop it fully, but you can slow it.) Kaya hindi mahahadlangan ng BSP ang oil shock, pero puwede nilang pigilin ang bilis ng paghina ng piso. (So the BSP can't block the oil shock, but they can curb how fast the peso weakens.) Perpekto. 四个词：究竟、反弹、bumawi、pigilin。(Perfect. Four words: jiūjìng, fǎntán, bumawi, pigilin.) Arts & Culture From currency defense to something much older and stranger. Arts and culture — and Alex, you've got a genuinely wild one to lead with. I do. In London, the Royal Academy opens "Richard Dadd: Beyond Bedlam" on July 25, running through October 25. And Dadd is one of the most extraordinary figures in Victorian art. Give people the story, because it's almost unbelievable. Dadd, born 1817, was a rising talent — and then, in the grip of a psychotic breakdown, he killed his father. He spent the rest of his life, until 1886, painting inside the asylums at Bethlem and Broadmoor. And what he produced there is astonishing — needle-sharp fairy paintings, obsessive in detail. The masterpiece is "The Fairy Feller's Master-Stroke," which he worked on from 1855 to 1864. And this is the first major reckoning with him in a long time. It's the most comprehensive show since the 1974 Tate survey — over 100 works, guest-curated by Nicholas Tromans, who wrote the definitive study. And the curatorial argument is the interesting part: it deliberately pushes past the "mad genius" cliché to make the case for the sheer technical virtuosity and narrative density of the work. The illness is context, not the whole story. From an asylum in Victorian England to a very loud summer in opera. Bayreuth's having a big year. An enormous one — it's the festival's 150th anniversary, opening July 24 and 25. Two headline events. First, Bayreuth's first-ever staging of Wagner's early opera "Rienzi" — which is a real curiosity, because it's traditionally not performed on that stage. And second, a brand-new "Ring" cycle billed as "RING 10010110," using AI-generated projections as, in their words, a generative visual force reflecting 150 years of interpretation. An AI "Ring." That will divide people. It will absolutely divide the Wagnerians. And down in Salzburg — which opened on the 17th under the theme "Panorama of Love" — there's a new "Carmen" premiering July 26 that's a genuine high-wire act. Asmik Grigorian sings the title role for the first time, Teodor Currentzis conducts, and it's staged and choreographed by Gabriela Carrizo of the Belgian dance-theatre troupe Peeping Tom, with Jonathan Tetelman as Don José. Grigorian doing Carmen for the first time, with a dance-theatre director — that's the kind of pairing that's either a triumph or a scandal. And no in-between. Both of those are previews — they open this week, so no reviews yet. Worth circling on the calendar. And on the page — Colson Whitehead closes out a trilogy. He does. "Cool Machine," out this month from Doubleday, is the third and final Ray Carney novel — the Harlem trilogy. It carries the furniture-salesman-and-fence Carney into Reaganomics-era New York, across 1981, 1983, and 1986. Publishers Weekly called it "the greatest New York novel in years." And this is a two-time Pulitzer winner landing the plane on a trilogy. Which is the hard thing to do well, and the early word is he sticks it. And one to hold for next week — the Booker Prize 2026 longlist drops on July 28, with a judging panel chaired by Mary Beard, alongside Patricia Lockwood and Raymond Antrobus. That'll be a conversation. Quick one to close the segment? Daniel Mason's "A Vermont Novel," out this month — his first since "North Woods" in 2023, which a lot of people loved. Early notices call it witty and gorgeous. A good stack. And speaking of things growing back — let's end on the good thing. One Good Thing Here's the one to leave you with — and it's a genuinely clever answer to one of the ugliest problems in materials science. Scientists have built a "living plastic" that self-destructs on command, and breaks down completely in six days — without leaving any microplastics behind. The microplastics part is the whole game, isn't it? Because normally when plastic "degrades," it just shatters into smaller and smaller pieces. That's exactly the trap this gets around. A team at the Shenzhen Institute of Synthetic Biology — published in ACS Applied Polymer Materials — engineered two cooperating strains of the bacterium Bacillus subtilis and baked them, in dormant spore form, right into the plastic film itself. So the microbes live inside the material. Dormant, until you trigger them. Add a warm nutrient broth — around 50 degrees Celsius — and the spores wake up and produce two complementary enzymes. One acts like a random chopper, slicing the long polymer chains into shorter pieces. The other is a patient nibbler, chewing those pieces from the ends all the way down to the base building blocks. And because the two work together, there's no intermediate microplastic stage. Right — that's the elegance of it. The material goes all the way back to monomers, not to a cloud of tiny fragments. They used polycaprolactone, which is common in 3D printing and some surgical sutures. Six days, fully gone, on command. A plastic that cleans up after itself. That's the kind of small, concrete win that actually compounds. It's engineering biology doing what it does best — building the solution into the problem. A lovely place to end. A good note to end on. That's The Morning Commute for Tuesday, July 21. Tonight the earnings jury comes back — Tesla, Alphabet, Intel — and we'll have the verdict tomorrow. Until then, take it easy, and we'll see you in the morning. 究竟 actually; after all; exactly (what) — an emphatic adverb that presses a question to its core referendum day — pressing the real question of what the market is actually worried about, chips or oil and rates 反弹 to rebound; to bounce back (of a price, index, or sentiment) off a low the week-long chip rout reversing — semis leading green on Monday bumawi to recoup; to win back or make up for something lost (root: bawi) the market recouping losses — chip stocks bouncing back off the week's rout pigilin to curb; to restrain or hold back a force already in motion (root: pigil) the BSP moving to curb peso volatility and rein in FX-derivative speculation

docs.title: Monday, July 20, 2026
docs.text: Monday, July 20, 2026 Cold Open Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Monday, July twentieth. I'm Sam. And I'm Alex. Here's the throughline for the week's open: the AI-stock wobble stopped being about one name and became about the whole complex. Friday was the third straight down day, and this time it broadened — the Dow, which had been the hiding place all week, fell too, and the selling spread clear across the Pacific into a China-tech rout. The trigger moved on, from a single model delay to a structural cost scare, with a Netflix growth crack on top. And underneath it, the same vise keeps tightening — oil bid on fresh Iran risk, yields stuck high, gold near 4,000 dollars, a live hold-versus-hike Fed just eight days out. Yet the physical build behind the whole trade only accelerates. Fundamentals roaring, prices flinching. Let's get into it. Headlines Headlines. Two from the world desk before we hit the tape, and the first is a genuine landmark out of Sudan. An army-aligned court has sentenced the Rapid Support Forces chief, Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo — the man everyone knows as Hemedti — to death, in absentia, for genocide and crimes against humanity in Darfur. Roughly a dozen to fifteen senior RSF figures were sentenced alongside him, including his own brothers, tied to the 2023 atrocities in el-Geneina and the killing of the West Darfur governor. And Britain layered fresh sanctions on top, targeting RSF commanders and the gold-and-finance networks bankrolling the war. A death sentence for a warlord who is nowhere near a courtroom. What does a verdict with no arrest power actually change? On the ground, not much tomorrow — there's no one to serve the warrant. But it hardens the split. It formally brands one side of this civil war a genocidal enterprise, which slams the door on the negotiated settlement diplomats keep reaching for, and it puts a legal target on the money. And the market-adjacent read is the gold. Sudan's war is financed through smuggled gold moving out through the Gulf, so tighter scrutiny of those chains keeps a risk premium on the whole Red Sea and Sahel corridor — the same corridor a lot of the world's shipping and a chunk of its informal gold trade run through. So a symbolic verdict with a very real financial tail. Second world story — and it's a fight inside the European Union itself. It is. Denmark, which took over the rotating EU Council presidency on the first of July, is formally pushing to trigger Article 7 — the so-called nuclear option — to strip Hungary of its voting rights in the bloc. The Danish minister Marie Bjerre is leading it, and the grievance is Viktor Orban's serial vetoes on Ukraine's accession, plus what Brussels calls core-values breaches on rule of law. Article 7 is the thing everyone always threatens and never does. Is this actually different? Procedurally it's still uphill — you need near-unanimity for the harshest step, and France and Germany haven't fully signed on, so an actual suspension isn't happening next week. But the fact that the presidency is driving it out in the open is the signal. This is really a proxy war over Ukraine. Orban has been the single point of failure blocking accession talks and tranches of aid, and if the bloc ever found a way to route around Budapest's veto, it re-rates everything Ukraine-exposed — the reconstruction names, the aid timeline, and the political-risk premium sitting on the euro. Watch it as a slow burn, not a same-day move. To the tape. Market Overview Friday's close, and it was ugly and — the important word — broad. The S&P 500 fell 1.01%, off 76 points to 7,457.69. The Nasdaq took the brunt again, down 1.4%, off 362 points to 25,520.24. But the tell was the Dow: down 0.77%, more than 400 points, to 52,146.42. On Thursday the Dow had barely moved while the Nasdaq bled — a clean growth-only story. Friday, the hiding place stopped hiding. So this is the part that matters. Two days ago it was a chips-and-growth problem. Friday it was a market problem. Exactly — the selling spread. That's the whole character change. When it's just the crowded AI names, you can call it a positioning unwind. When the Dow gives back 400 points on the same day, the de-grossing has widened into something closer to a broad risk-off. And the proximate trigger evolved with it. Thursday it was Alphabet and a model delay. Friday it was structural: TSMC guided capital spending higher than the street wanted, which spooked everyone about the cost curve of the whole AI build, and — the sharper one — a Chinese lab, Moonshot, dropped a model they're calling Kimi K3, billed as the largest openly downloadable open-weight model yet. And an open-weight model that good is a dagger at the "more compute forever" thesis. That's precisely why the semis took it hardest. If a free, downloadable model closes the gap on the frontier, the market starts asking whether the trillion-dollar compute build is as defensible as the multiples assume. AMD was down 5% intraday, Intel off 4%, Nvidia down 3% before trimming to close off 2.2% at 202.81. The whole megacap block went with it — Meta down 2.8%, Tesla off 2.6%, Alphabet down 2.2%. The philadelphia semiconductor index is now off 13% in a month. And the single-name gut-punch was Netflix. It was — down 7.3% Friday to a fifty-two-week low, and it traded down as much as 12% at the worst. The print itself was fine: earnings 80 cents, revenue 12.56 billion, up 13%. What did the damage was the guide — third-quarter revenue guided to 12.86 billion, a hair light of the 13 billion the street wanted — plus a decision to stop reporting engagement data twice a year and only disclose it annually. So they dimmed the lights on their own metrics in the exact week the market has zero tolerance for it. Zero tolerance is right. In this tape, any growth name that decelerates and then reduces disclosure gets taken to the woodshed. It's the same reflex that hit Alphabet — the market is repricing every richly-valued growth story at the first hint the trajectory is flattening. Netflix is now on track for its worst year since 2022. And underneath the equity drama, the rates and commodity picture is the part that should still unsettle people. This is the crux, and it hasn't changed — it's gotten more acute. The 10-year sits at 4.55%, the 2-year at 4.18%, the 30-year at 5.06%. Yields are not coming down to rescue equities, and the reason is oil. West Texas crude jumped 4.5% on Friday to about 82 and a half dollars, Brent up near 88, on fresh US-Iran violence and the same Hormuz shipping risk we keep circling. So the bond market is looking straight through Wednesday's cool June inflation print — 3.5% headline, 2.6% core — to a forward oil chart that just turned higher again. And gold, with a live war and a shaky stock market, is doing what? Sitting around 4,030 dollars and going nowhere — which is itself the message. When gold can't catch a bid with equities falling and missiles flying, it's telling you high real yields and a firm dollar are the dominant force, not fear. The dollar's strong, the yen is pinned at a shockingly weak 162.4 to the dollar, near its cycle low. Rates are running this market. So what does all of that do to the Fed, eight days out? It boxes them further in. The July meeting is the twenty-eighth and twenty-ninth, no new dot plot attached. And the debate is not hold-versus-cut anymore — it's hold-versus-hike. Markets have a hold as the overwhelming base case, but the only alternative anyone is pricing is a hike, roughly a one-in-five tail, because core is stuck at 2.6% and the oil bid threatens to reverse this month's energy relief. A cut is simply not in the conversation. Constructive on earnings, cautious on rates, and every model still runs through the price of a barrel. US business. U.S. Business US business — two earnings from Friday, and both cut to a theme we've been tracking. Start with the banks. Fifth Third. Second-quarter net income 763 million dollars, GAAP earnings 83 cents — that actually missed the street's 84 by a penny — but adjusted earnings were 1.02. The number that matters is net interest income: up 48% year on year to 2.22 billion. Because this was the first full quarter with Comerica folded in — the 10.9 billion-dollar acquisition that closed earlier this year and made Fifth Third the ninth-largest bank in the country. So the marquee regional-bank merger of the cycle is finally showing up in the financials. And that's the story — the deal thesis was always scale and a bigger interest-margin engine, and you can see the engine in that 48% NII jump. The penny miss on the headline is the flip side: integration costs and one-time charges still weigh while you're bolting two banks together. But this is the number regional-bank investors wanted — proof the consolidation wave is translating into earnings power, not just press releases. In a year when the mid-cap banks needed a reason to re-rate, a working merger is it. Second name — and it was the rare clean beat on a filthy day. Travelers, and it was a blowout. Core income of 10.04 a share against a street around 5.33 — call it an 88% beat. Net income 2.2 billion, revenue 12.15 billion. And the number the whole insurance complex watches, the combined ratio, came in at 83.6% — anything under 100 is an underwriting profit, and the low 80s is exceptional. What drove a beat that size — is it real, or is it one-off? A bit of both, honestly. Catastrophe losses ran about half what they did a year ago — a quieter quarter for storms — and they booked a 578 million-dollar favorable reserve release, which means prior years' claims came in cheaper than they'd set aside. That's not repeatable every quarter. But the underlying underwriting margin is genuinely strong, and here's why it fits the day: on a tape punishing every growth name that so much as blinks, a boring property-casualty insurer printed a huge beat and was one of the only large caps green on Friday, next to Exxon and Apple. Money leaving the crowded AI trade has to land somewhere, and Friday it went to the compounder that beat. Two financials, both telling you where the safety is right now. International. International Business & Politics Two from abroad, and the first keeps building one of the year's cleanest secular trades. Saab — the Swedish defense group — beat on the second quarter Friday, and the headline is the backlog: a record order book, powered by a Polish submarine order that added something like 47 billion Swedish kronor, roughly 7 billion dollars, of new bookings in the quarter alone. The chief executive used the results to lecture Europe about overhauling how it buys weapons. So the European rearmament story isn't slowing — it's compounding. It's compounding, and that's the point to hold. We've flagged the continental defense build as a structural, multi-year capex cycle, and a record backlog at Saab is the hard evidence — order books that stretch years out, not a single good quarter. It's why the defense primes — Saab, Rheinmetall, BAE Systems — keep re-rating even on days the broad market is falling apart. When the tape sells off but the backlog names hold, that's the market telling you which themes it believes are real. Second one — Airbus, and it's really a China-and-geopolitics story wearing an aviation jacket. Nicely put. Airbus landed a fresh round of orders from Chinese state carriers on Friday — Air China taking 15 A350-900 widebodies, Shenzhen Airlines 40 of the A320neo family. And the subtext is everything: China is steering its aircraft demand to Europe while Boeing is mired in its own troubles. So Beijing is using plane orders as a diplomatic instrument. Exactly — aircraft orders are one of the most visible geopolitical bargaining chips there is, because each one is billions of dollars and thousands of jobs, and Beijing knows precisely which capitals feel it. Tilting that demand toward Toulouse and away from an American manufacturer is a message wrapped in a purchase order. For Airbus it's a real win — it extends their dominance in the single most important growth market in aviation, and it deepens their backlog at exactly the moment Boeing can least afford to lose ground. China to close the block. China And China is where Friday's selloff went to get worse. While Wall Street was falling, the Chinese tech complex was in a genuine rout: the Shanghai Composite down 3.1%, the STAR 50 — Shanghai's answer to the Nasdaq — off a brutal 7.1%, and Hong Kong's tech index down more than 4%. This wasn't a spillover from New York. It was its own bubble getting a leg cut out. And the backdrop is almost surreal, because Beijing was simultaneously throwing itself a party for AI. That's the contradiction of the whole thing. All this happened around the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, where Xi Jinping gave a speech pushing global AI cooperation, and China and 28 other nations signed on to a new Shanghai-based body — they're calling it the World AI Cooperation Organization, an explicit bid to write the world's AI-governance rules from Shanghai rather than Washington. And under that official optimism, the froth was extreme: Zhipu's latest GLM model, a startup called MiniMax teasing a 2.7-trillion-parameter monster, and Moonshot's Kimi K3 — the same open-weight model that helped knock the American semis down. So the same model that spooked Nvidia spooked its own home market harder. Which is the sharp read. The Chinese AI names had run on pure euphoria — governance leadership, model-size bragging rights, national-champion status — and when the market started to ask whether any of it makes money, the de-rating was violent, far worse than the US version. And it lands right on top of the two-speed economy we keep describing: a technology sector priced for a revolution sitting on top of a real economy with soft consumption and stubborn deflationary pressure. Beijing wants to lead the world on AI rules while its own AI stocks just told you what happens when narrative meets valuation. That's the block. To the desk this whole show keeps circling back to. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities Segment two — the energy desk. And Alex, here's the thing that makes today's data so striking: while the AI stocks were being cut apart, the physical evidence that the build is accelerating got more concrete, not less. Start with the capacity auction. Start with PJM, because the number is almost poetic in how broken it is. PJM — the biggest grid operator in America, serving 65 million people — ran its 2028-2029 capacity auction, and the results landed Monday the fourteenth. It cleared roughly 138,000 megawatts, and it cleared at 325 dollars per megawatt-day — which is the legislated price cap. The absolute ceiling. Third auction in a row pinned to its own cap. And clearing at the cap sounds bullish for generators, but you're telling me it's actually a broken signal. It's a broken signal, and that's the whole insight. Look underneath: the auction cleared about 6.8 gigawatts below the reliability requirement — it literally could not buy enough capacity to meet the target — and it drew only about 500 megawatts of genuinely new supply. FERC's new chair, Laura Swett, said that out loud on the sixteenth. So the market is screaming scarcity at maximum price, and the response is 500 megawatts of new build. A functioning market clears higher and summons supply. This one has hit its ceiling and summoned almost nothing. So the price mechanism has stopped doing its job. It's stopped doing its job, and that's the story of the whole sector right now. When the capacity market can't call forth new plants even at its legal maximum, scarcity stops being a price-discovery problem and becomes a governance problem — which is exactly why the action is moving to Washington. And that brings us to the single most important thing happening today, literally today, that almost nobody outside this desk is watching. Which is? Today, July twentieth, is the deadline for all six of the country's grid operators — PJM, MISO, SPP, California, New England, and New York — to file generation-adequacy reports with FERC, under show-cause orders FERC issued back on June eighteenth. This is the federal government stepping in to standardize the rules for how these enormous data-center loads connect to the grid, and how co-located power — a data center bolting its own gas plant on behind the meter — gets treated across every market at once. So instead of every state and every utility improvising, Washington is trying to write one rulebook. One rulebook, and the stakes are the cost-shift. FERC named five reform priorities, and the big one is protecting ordinary ratepayers from subsidizing the grid upgrades these hyperscalers trigger — plus a formal study process for generators sitting "electrically proximate" to a large load. Get this right and the behind-the-meter gas deals get a clean, repeatable tariff path. Get it wrong and every one of them stays bespoke and litigated for years. The outcome quietly decides how fast the whole build can actually plug in. And put the scale of the demand next to the scale of the supply, because that gap is the investable fact. Bank of America did exactly that mid-month, and the numbers are stark. Data centers alone add about 125 gigawatts of US electricity load between now and 2030. Total power demand growing at a 4.1% compound rate — which sounds small until you remember US power demand was essentially flat for two decades. And here's the gap: BofA models more than 230 gigawatts of capacity demand against roughly 93 gigawatts of supply. Call it a 100-gigawatt shortfall by 2030. A hundred gigawatts short. That's not a rounding error, that's a hundred large power plants that don't exist. And the binding constraint is not money — it's turbines. Large gas turbines are sold out through 2030. You cannot buy a slot. So developers are pivoting to on-site gas engines — reciprocating engines, the kind that can be delivered in quarters not years — while utilities quietly extend the life of coal plants, bolt on batteries, and race to build transmission. When the constraint is a manufacturing queue and not capital, the whole game becomes who has secured their megawatts, physically, on paper, today. And you can see that in how the deals are now being structured. You can, and it's the sharpest change in the market. Look at the joint venture that launched on the fifteenth — Coravel, a fifty-fifty between the Spanish builder ACS and BlackRock's infrastructure arm. Their very first act was to sign a hyperscaler lease — about 140 megawatts of critical IT load at a Dallas-Fort Worth campus, with rights to another 100 on top, and a pipeline of 1.7 gigawatts behind it. And the model is the point: they wrap the power, the building, and the operations into a single contract. Power, shell, and operations sold as one product. That's a direct threat to the plain-vanilla data-center landlord. It's an existential threat to the standalone developer who can only offer the building and says "go find your own electricity." When power is the scarce input, whoever controls the megawatts controls the deal — and the capital pooling into this, BlackRock-scale capital, is betting the integrated model wins. The land is no longer the asset. The deliverable megawatt is the asset. And that scarcity is exactly what turns this into the cost-of-living fight we keep flagging. It is, and the concentration is the trigger. Digital Realty just locked up 1,440 acres near Kansas City — but the gating piece wasn't the land, it was a signed energy agreement with the local utility for 600 megawatts by early 2028, scaling to 2 gigawatts. And in one rural Missouri county, Montgomery County, Amazon and Google announced separate campuses — 10 billion dollars from one, 15 from the other. 25 billion dollars of hyperscale capex dropped into a single county's utility footprint. And that whole cost lands on one small utility and its existing customers. That's the powder keg. When you concentrate that much new load on one utility, the grid upgrades to serve it get spread across everybody's bill — and that's precisely the ratebase fight that started in Virginia and Pennsylvania and is now going nationwide. It's why FERC's rulebook today matters so much: the entire question is whether grandmother's power bill subsidizes a hyperscaler, or the hyperscaler pays for the capacity it causes. The stocks wobbled on Friday. The build did not. Bring it home to the Philippines. Philippines Segment three — the Philippines, where that same oil bid we keep tracking lands as a very direct squeeze. Weather first, and the good news is it's quiet. Quiet for once. As of Sunday and into today, PAGASA has no tropical cyclone in the country, no low-pressure area worth naming, and no wind signals up anywhere. What's active is just the habagat — the southwest monsoon — bringing isolated afternoon downpours to the western side of the country. Over the weekend the rain focused on the far north of Luzon: Ilocos, Zambales, Batanes, the Babuyan Islands, Apayao, Abra — the usual flash-flood and landslide watch during the heavy cells, but nothing widespread or prolonged. Metro Manila hit about 33 and a half degrees today. A rare calm week on the weather map. So the story is the macro, and the macro is a squeeze. It is, and it starts with the central bank. The BSP is holding its policy rate at 4.75% after hiking a quarter-point in June — its second straight hike. And it turned more hawkish, not less: it raised its own 2026 inflation forecast to 6.4%, and pegged 2027 at 4.5% — both above the 2-to-4% target band. Governor Remolona called June's move "pre-emptive" and pointedly did not rule out more. The street — MUFG, Nomura, Citi — is penciling in another 50 basis points of hikes ahead. A central bank hiking into a slowdown. That only happens when it's defending something. It's defending the currency and fighting imported inflation — and you can see why in the prints. Headline inflation did cool to 6.4% in June from 6.8%, which sounds like relief. But look under it: core inflation actually rose, to 4.4% from 4.1%. The cooling was all transport and food — the volatile stuff — while the underlying, stickier pressure got worse. That's the worst kind of inflation mix for a central banker: the headline improves, the core hardens. And the peso is the pressure gauge. The peso is sitting at 61.67 to the dollar — a hair off the record low around 61.75 it touched earlier in the week, and down more than 4% on the year. And the mechanism is brutally simple and ties straight to our throughline: the Philippines imports almost all of its oil, so every dollar that Hormuz and the Iran risk adds to crude widens the import bill, pressures the peso, and feeds the very inflation the BSP is fighting. You can see it in the trade numbers — the May trade deficit blew out to 5.5 billion dollars, more than half again wider than a year ago, with fuel imports alone at 1.75 billion in the month. So it's the global oil story with a national price tag attached. That's exactly it — a fuel-importing economy absorbing an oil shock with a currency near a record low and a central bank forced to keep tightening into it. The BSP is leaning on reserves to smooth the peso rather than spend all its rate ammunition at once. It's a holding action: defend the currency, keep policy tight, and hope the crude bid fades before patience does. And there's a big political week landing on top of it — President Marcos delivers his fifth State of the Nation Address next Monday, the twenty-seventh, and the cost of living is going to be the whole subtext, because families feel this oil-and-peso squeeze at the pump and in the market basket every single day. Structurally intact, cyclically squeezed. Onward — and this next one is my favorite. Vocab Of The Day Segment four — vocab of the day. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, all tied to today's stories, and we do this one in-language. Alex, start us in Mandarin. 第一个词是"然而",意思是"但是、可是",不过它更正式、更书面。造个句子:AI 的建设还在加速,然而相关股票却连跌三天。(The first word is "rán'ér," meaning "however, yet" — but it's more formal, more written. A sentence: the AI buildout keeps accelerating, however the related stocks have fallen three days straight.) 所以"然而"跟"但是"意思一样,只是语气不同?(So "rán'ér" means the same as "dànshì," just a different register?) 对,意思几乎一样,但"然而"用在书面语、财经评论里,"但是"更口语。(Right, nearly the same meaning, but "rán'ér" belongs to written language and financial commentary, while "dànshì" is more colloquial.) 它常常引出一个跟前面相反的、让你意外的结果。(It often introduces a result that contradicts what came before, one that surprises you.) 基本面很强,然而市场并不买账。(The fundamentals are strong; however, the market isn't buying it.) 我懂了——用"然而"听起来更像财经社论。第二个词呢?(I see — using "rán'ér" sounds more like a financial editorial. And the second word?) 第二个词是"蔓延",本义是像火或藤蔓一样扩散,现在多用来形容负面情绪或危机的扩散。(The second word is "mànyán" — literally to spread like fire or a creeping vine, now mostly used for the spread of negative sentiment or a crisis.) 星期五,恐慌从科技股蔓延到整个市场,甚至蔓延到亚洲。(On Friday, the panic spread from tech stocks to the whole market, and even spread across to Asia.) "蔓延"和"扩大"有什么不同?(What's the difference between "mànyán" and "kuòdà," to expand?) 好问题。"扩大"是主动地、有意地把范围做大,比如扩大投资;"蔓延"是失控地、自己往外爬,通常是坏事。(Good question. "Kuòdà" is to enlarge a scope deliberately, on purpose — like expanding investment; "mànyán" is uncontrolled, creeping outward on its own, and usually it's something bad.) 所以我们说恐慌蔓延、危机蔓延、抛售蔓延——绝不会说"恐慌扩大"那么自然。(So we say panic spreads, crisis spreads, the selloff spreads — you'd never say "panic expands" as naturally.) 这是书面语,新闻里到处都是。(It's written register, and it's everywhere in the news.) 太贴切了,正好是今天的行情。现在换菲律宾语。(Perfectly apt — that's exactly today's market. Now let's switch to Tagalog.) Ang unang salita ay "lumaganap" — ang kumalat nang malawakan, parang apoy o balita na kumakalat sa lahat ng dako. (The first word is "lumaganap" — to spread widely, like a fire or news spreading everywhere.) Noong Biyernes, mabilis na lumaganap ang benta mula sa Wall Street hanggang sa Asya. (On Friday, the selloff spread fast from Wall Street all the way to Asia.) Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "lumaganap" sa "kumalat"? (What's the difference between "lumaganap" and "kumalat"?) Magkalapit sila, pero ang "kumalat" ay pang-araw-araw — kumalat ang tsismis, kumalat ang sakit. Ang "lumaganap" ay mas malawak, mas pormal ang dating — paglaganap ng teknolohiya, paglaganap ng krisis. (They're close, but "kumalat" is everyday — a rumor spreads, a disease spreads. "Lumaganap" is broader and sounds more formal — the spread of technology, the spread of a crisis.) Ito ang katumbas ng Mandarin na 蔓延 na kasama natin kanina. (It's the counterpart of the Mandarin "mànyán" we just had.) Magandang tulay 'yan. At ang huling salita? (Nice bridge. And the last word?) "Tiisin" — mula sa "tiis," ang matiyagang pagbata sa hirap o sakit nang hindi sumusuko. Tinitiis ng ekonomiya ng Pilipinas ang pagsipa ng langis at ang paghina ng piso. (The word is "tiisin," from "tiis" — to bear hardship or pain patiently without giving up. The Philippine economy is enduring the surge in oil and the weakening of the peso.) Iba ba ito sa "batahin"? (Is this different from "batahin," to carry a load?) Oo. Ang "batahin" ay pagbubuhat ng bigat; ang "tiisin" ay pagtitiis — may tapang at pasensya, kahit masakit, nagpapatuloy ka pa rin. (Yes. "Batahin" is to lift a weight; "tiisin" is to endure — there's courage and patience in it, and even when it hurts, you keep going.) Isang salita na puno ng damdaming Pilipino: ang matiisin. (One word full of Filipino feeling — the quality of being long-suffering, "matiisin.") Language living inside the news. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog — a formal "however," a crisis that spreads, a selloff spreading wide, and an economy enduring. That's today's show in four words. Couldn't have scripted the throughline better. Arts & Culture Segment five — arts and culture. And we lead with the galleries today, not the box office. Alex, there's a major reappraisal opening in London. A big one, at Tate Modern — a career survey of Ana Mendieta, the Cuban-American artist, her first major UK show in over a decade. More than 120 works: the famous Silueta series, where she pressed the silhouette of her own body into earth, sand, and fire and photographed the trace; newly remastered films; and rarely seen early paintings and late sculptures. It runs into January. Mendieta is a name a lot of people know for the tragedy of how she died, more than for the work itself. And that's exactly what this show sets out to correct. For years her death — the fall from a New York window in 1985, and the acquittal of her husband, the sculptor Carl Andre — has overshadowed the art. What Tate is doing is restoring the films and the objects behind those iconic photographs, and arguing that she was central, not marginal, to how we now think about the body, gender, and land in art. It's a reframing of the canon, not just a retrospective — putting a woman who worked with earth and absence back at the center of the story. And there's a quieter rediscovery on at the National Gallery too. There is, and it's a lovely counterpoint — Waldmüller: Landscapes. Ferdinand Georg Waldmüller was an Austrian Biedermeier painter, early 1800s, and this is the first-ever UK exhibition devoted to him, done with the Belvedere in Vienna. The argument the show makes is that he was painting hard, clear, sunstruck Alpine light out of doors decades before the French Impressionists got the credit for it. A realist ahead of his moment, finally getting his English-language hearing. From canvas to the concert hall — the summer festivals are in full swing. They are, and the standout was the First Night of the BBC Proms on Friday, at the Royal Albert Hall. Dalia Stasevska conducted the BBC Symphony in an all-American program for the United States' 250th year — Copland's Fanfare rolling into Gershwin's An American in Paris. But the moment everyone wrote about was the young pianist Yunchan Lim playing Ravel's Piano Concerto in G. The critics used words like "startling assurance" — the way he balanced the percussive bite of the outer movements against the aching lyricism of that slow middle movement. Lim is the one who won the Van Cliburn a few years back at eighteen, isn't he — the phenomenon. The very same, and this is him proving the phenomenon has real depth. And there's a wall of opera behind it if you want it — Salzburg opened the same night with a new Carmen, Asmik Grigorian in the title role under Teodor Currentzis, and Bayreuth's 150th-anniversary season opens later this week. A rich few days for anyone who follows the voice. And one for the readers to close: Sigrid Nunez — the National Book Award winner behind The Friend — has just published her first-ever story collection, called It Will Come Back to You. Thirteen stories gathered across her whole career. And the reviews on that? Warm — critics are saying her trademark wit and quiet empathy are actually sharper in miniature than at novel length, plot turns so gentle they barely register as turns, and never a slide into sentiment. And the timing's neat, because the book world is holding its breath for next Tuesday, the twenty-eighth, when the 2026 Booker Prize longlist drops — a panel chaired, wonderfully, by the classicist Mary Beard. So a good week to have a novelist's short stories on the nightstand while the big list gets argued over. One good thing to finish. One Good Thing And to close — one good thing, and it's a slow-motion miracle hiding in plain sight. It's the forests of New England. A scientist at the Woodwell Climate Research Center, Bill Moomaw, has been pointing at a fact most people who live there never notice: Massachusetts, which was roughly 90% cleared of its forest by the mid-1800s — stripped for farms and timber — is now back to about 60% forest cover. New England as a whole was logged down by around 80%, and it has quietly regrown. Moomaw calls it, flatly, "the greatest forest recovery in the history of the world." And nobody planted it — this is the land just taking itself back. That's the wonderful part. When the farms moved west and the fields were abandoned, the forest simply walked back in on its own, tree by tree, over a century and a half — no grand restoration program, no ribbon-cutting. And now those regrown woods are pulling carbon out of the air, holding soil, cooling towns, and sheltering wildlife that had nowhere to go. It's a reminder that nature's default setting, given half a chance and a little time, is to heal. A century and a half of quiet regrowth. A good one to start the week on. That's your Morning Commute for Monday. Whatever this week has in store — — take it steady, look after each other, and we'll be right back here tomorrow. Have a good one. 然而 however; yet; nevertheless (formal contrastive connective) the gap between a roaring AI buildout and a third straight down day in AI stocks 蔓延 to spread; to creep outward (like fire or a vine) — now mostly of negative sentiment or a crisis Friday's selloff spreading from tech to the whole market and across to Asia lumaganap to spread widely; to proliferate (of news, fire, a crisis) the selloff spreading from Wall Street across to Asian markets tiisin to endure; to bear hardship or pain patiently without giving up (root: tiis) the Philippine economy absorbing the oil shock and a near-record-low peso

docs.title: Friday, July 17, 2026
docs.text: Friday, July 17, 2026 Cold Open Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Friday, July seventeenth. I'm Sam. And I'm Alex. The throughline today: the AI trade just took its second straight down day, and the tell is who's getting punished — the growth names slipping on their guides. Alphabet led it lower on a model delay. Meanwhile the same oil bid that's keeping the Fed boxed in is showing up as real costs everywhere else: a 6-billion-dollar fuel hit one airline just swallowed, a data-center line item landing on ordinary power bills, and a rare-earth chokehold with a trillion-dollar price tag on it. Let's get into it. Headlines Headlines. World desk first — the Middle East, still setting the tempo for everything downstream. The war's into its second week with no ceasefire in sight, and the market-relevant fact is the chokepoint. Iran's keeping the pressure on shipping through Hormuz, tanker traffic is running thin, insurers are repricing war-risk cover, and crude is holding its bid — Brent up around the mid-80s, near one-month highs, with the market still carrying a tail toward 90 if a tanker or a terminal actually goes. And that's the whole macro pivot we've been living in — the war premium that bled out in June is back in the curve. It is, and the point today is the second-order damage, not the headline count. And it isn't just crude — watch the refined products, because that's where households and airlines actually feel it. Diesel and jet-fuel cracks widen when refiners fear feedstock disruption, so the pump price and the airfare can move even before the barrel does. Higher crude flows into corporate fuel bills, into freight and shipping rates, into the Philippine import tab, into a Fed that can't declare victory on inflation. We'll hit all three today. The oil chart is still the single most important input on the board, and it's being set in a strait most listeners will never see. And the risk asymmetry from here? Skewed to the upside on price. The spot move has mostly happened; what's cheap is the optionality on a genuine supply disruption. So you don't chase crude here — you own the tail. And every desk is watching the same thing: does OPEC-plus lean in with spare barrels to cap it, or defend price and let it run. That single decision sets the inflation path for the back half of the year. Second world story, and it's a leadership shock in Kyiv. Zelensky cleaned house. He dismissed his defense minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, and parliament moved to approve a new wartime government — hundreds turned out to protest in Kyiv. The reporting ties it to a doctrine clash with the army chief, Oleksandr Syrsky: Fedorov was the asymmetric-warfare bet, the drones and the cyber, and Syrsky wants the resources going to holding territory. So it's a strategy fight dressed as a personnel move, at a terrible moment to be re-litigating strategy. Exactly — re-arguing doctrine mid-war. The market read is narrow but real: it matters for how durable Western resolve looks into the autumn, and for the European defense-capex theme that's been one of the year's cleaner secular trades. Instability atop Kyiv's command is not what the re-armament complex wants to see priced. Onto markets. Market Overview Thursday's close, and it was a second straight down day led by the AI complex. The S&P 500 off 51 basis points to 7,533.77, the Nasdaq down a heavier 1.5% to 25,881.95, and the Dow far more resilient — off just 20 basis points, down 105.67 points, to 52,552.97. So a growth-and-chips problem, not a market problem. The Dow barely moved. Right. Under the hood the breadth wasn't nearly as ugly as the Nasdaq headline — it was concentrated in the biggest, most crowded AI names. And the proximate trigger was Alphabet, down more than 4% after Bloomberg reported it's behind schedule delivering Gemini 3.5 Pro, its flagship model. A model delay knocks four percent off a company that size? When it's priced for AI leadership, yes. The whole terminal-value story for these names rests on staying at the frontier, so any slip in the roadmap reprices the multiple at the margin — and it pulled the semis and the memory names down with it, SanDisk and Western Digital among the hardest hit. That's the week in one trade: no forgiveness for any crack in the AI-growth story. And the rates picture is the part that should still surprise people. This is the crux. June CPI landed Tuesday at 3.5% headline, below the 3.8% consensus, month-over-month minus four-tenths — the biggest drop since April 2020, almost entirely energy. Core 2.6%, flat. On paper, a green light to cut. And yet the 10-year sits around 4.57%, the 2-year near 4.17% — yields ticked up, not down. Which is the counterintuitive bit. Cooler inflation, higher yields. Because the bond market is looking straight through a backward-looking, energy-driven print to a forward crude chart that just turned higher. The disinflation in that number is the very oil that's already reversing. So the July meeting — the FOMC's the twenty-eighth and twenty-ninth — isn't hold-versus-cut anymore. It's hold-versus-hike. Wednesday's PPI, minus three-tenths on the month, tells the same story: soft realized inflation, hard forward risk. And the positioning underneath the tape? Because two down days in the crowded names smells like more than fundamentals. It is. What you're watching is a de-grossing in the most consensus book on the street. Everybody owns the same handful of AI winners, so when one cracks, the risk managers trim the whole basket at once — that's why a single-name model delay becomes a sector-wide 1.5% down day. It's mechanical, not a change of thesis. The equal-weight index held up far better than the cap-weight, which is the classic signature of a positioning unwind rather than a growth scare. So a crowded-trade wobble, not a top. That's the base case — a crowded trade shaking out weak hands, not the end of the theme. But it's fragile precisely because it's so concentrated. When the top seven names are this much of the index, the market's health and a handful of AI roadmaps are the same sentence. And the cross-asset tells? The interesting one is gold — around 4,000 dollars and drifting lower even with a live war, because a firmer dollar and higher real yields are outmuscling the haven bid. The dollar index up near 100 and a half. When gold can't rally on a Middle-East escalation, that tells you rates are the dominant force right now, not fear. And the backdrop under all of it? Earnings, and they're the cushion. FactSet has Q2 S&P growth tracking north of 23%, revised up from about 19% at quarter start — an unusually large upward revision mid-season. So the E in the multiple is compounding even as the macro gets noisier. So what actually breaks the standoff — the cool inflation or the hot oil? Oil, in the near term. If crude keeps climbing into the meeting, the July inflation print reverses this month's energy relief and the hold-to-hike debate tips hawkish for real. If the barrel fades, the disinflation trend reasserts and the cut comes back onto the table for the autumn. It genuinely is that binary, which is why the whole desk is watching the Strait of Hormuz instead of the dot plot. Net it out: constructive on earnings, cautious on rates, and every model runs through the price of a barrel. US business — and it's a tale of two guides. U.S. Business US business. Two earnings that dropped after yesterday's close, and both cut straight to today's throughline. Start with the airline. United Airlines. Adjusted earnings of 1 dollar 99 a share, revenue 17.7 billion, up 16%, premium revenue up 16%, cargo up 23%, a pre-tax margin near 6%. And here's the line that matters: they raised full-year adjusted EPS guidance to 9-to-11 dollars — while absorbing nearly 6 billion dollars of extra fuel cost this year. The fuel bill alone was up 2.3 billion in the quarter, 84%, worth more than a dollar a share. So this is the oil story with a P&L attached. A company eating the crude spike in real time and still guiding up. That's exactly why it's the tell. The demand side — premium cabins, corporate travel, international, cargo — is strong enough to eat an 84% jump in jet fuel and still raise the number. It tells you the consumer at the top end is intact, and it tells you where the oil shock actually lands first: on margins, quietly, not on demand. If crude keeps climbing, that 6-billion becomes the headline instead of the footnote, and the guide gets a lot harder to defend. Second name — UnitedHealth, and it was a monster. A blowout. Adjusted earnings of 6 dollars 38 against about 4.90 expected, revenue 112 billion, and the number the whole managed-care complex watches — the medical loss ratio — improved to 86.7%. They raised full-year adjusted EPS to 19.50-to-20 dollars, and the stock jumped around 7%. After the year that sector's had, a clean beat on the loss ratio is a real signal. It's the signal. The bear case on managed care all year was that medical costs were running away from them — utilization normalizing higher, seniors using more care. A loss ratio back under 87% with raised guidance says they've got their arms around it again. And notice the rotation it captures: in a tape punishing every growth name that decelerates, here's a defensive healthcare giant re-rating higher on execution. Money leaving the crowded AI trade has to go somewhere, and yesterday it went into the boring compounder that beat. And the read-through for the rest of the group? That's why it's a market event, not just a company event — UnitedHealth is the bellwether, so a loss-ratio beat de-risks the read into the rest of the managed-care names reporting over the next two weeks. If the biggest, most complex book in the industry got costs under control, the smaller players probably did too. It's the kind of print that quietly resets an entire sector's multiple. Two guides, two directions — the whole market in a pair of earnings. International next. International Business & Politics Two across the Atlantic. First, pharma M&A: Novartis is buying the UK biotech Myricx Bio for up to 1.5 billion dollars — 1.1 billion upfront, up to 400 million in milestones. It's a next-generation antibody-drug-conjugate platform, a novel payload chemistry, with lead oncology assets aimed at B7-H3 and HER2. Closing expected in the second half. Another big-pharma bolt-on into the ADC race. And that's the pattern to hold. Antibody-drug conjugates are the hottest modality in oncology — a targeted warhead that delivers chemo straight to the tumor — and with the patent cliffs looming late-decade, the majors are buying the next platform rather than waiting to invent it. Small, targeted, science-led deals. When organic growth is scarce, the balance sheets go shopping, and biotech is where they shop. Second, a genuine political milestone in Britain — Labour has its new leader. Andy Burnham is set to be elected Labour leader today, and to replace Starmer as prime minister on Monday. We flagged this as a slow-burn gilt story when Starmer stepped down; now it's real. Burnham runs to Starmer's left, so the market question is fiscal — does he reopen the spending envelope Rachel Reeves spent two years defending? And the gilt market's answer so far? No gap move yet — this isn't a Truss-style event, there's no fiscal statement attached to the handover. But this is exactly the kind of thing that sits under the long end for months. If the market starts to sniff a looser fiscal stance under a Burnham government, it re-rates the 30-year first, and it shows up in sterling. Watch the gilt curve into his first budget, whenever that lands. A change of prime minister on Monday is a slow-burn risk, not a same-day one. China to close the block. China The fresh China story isn't a data print — it's leverage. The IEA's new Global Critical Minerals Outlook, out yesterday, warns that if China fully implements its expanded rare-earth export controls, it could put roughly 6.5 trillion dollars of downstream Western production at risk — autos, high-tech, defense, and clean energy. 6.5 trillion. Put that number in context, because it's enormous. It's the estimated value of all the manufacturing that depends on those inputs — the magnets and motors inside an EV, a wind turbine, a fighter jet, a data-center cooling system. Beijing broadened the controls last October and then delayed full implementation by a year, and that delay is the tell: it's a loaded weapon sitting on the table through the trade talks. And the exposure isn't evenly shared — the US and Europe would absorb nearly half the hit. So it's the mirror image of the AI-power story we keep telling. Instead of being short electrons, the West is short the minerals. That's the sharp read, and it's why this is more than a headline. The entire re-industrialization push — reshoring, defense rebuild, the energy transition — runs on a supply chain China dominates end to end, from the mine to the refined magnet. This report is just the IEA putting a number on the chokehold. It reframes every reshoring announcement: you can permit the factory, pour the concrete, and still not have the neodymium to run it. Strategic dependency with a price tag attached. And we've seen the dress rehearsal for how China uses this. We have — the gallium and germanium curbs a couple of years back were the proof of concept, targeted controls that sent Western buyers scrambling and prices spiking. Rare earths are that same playbook at a hundred times the scale, because there's no quick substitute and no Western refining capacity that can spin up in under a decade. The investable read is that anything tied to ex-China supply — a Western magnet maker, a rare-earth miner, a recycler — carries a strategic premium now that has nothing to do with this quarter's earnings and everything to do with who controls the inputs. That's the block. Changing gears to the desk this whole show keeps circling. Energy / Data Centers / Utilities Segment two — the energy desk. And Alex, the fresh angle today isn't the queue or the auction we keep tracking. It's the bill. It's the bill, and it just went from theory to a number on a statement. PPL in Pennsylvania raised residential rates 4.9% on July first — about 7 dollars and 42 cents a month on a thousand-kilowatt-hour bill — and, crucially, it created a new large-load "data-center" rate class. That's the first Pennsylvania utility to try to wall off data-center costs from ordinary ratepayers. Which is the fight we said would turn political the moment it hit people's wallets. And now it has. For contrast, take Virginia — the data-center capital of the world — where a resident's January bill hit 281 dollars, roughly three times normal. So you've got two models crystallizing in real time. Model one, the Virginia path: the grid upgrades for hyperscaler load get socialized into everyone's bill. Model two, the new Pennsylvania path: you create a separate rate class and make the data centers pay for the capacity they cause. Every state regulator in the country is about to have to choose between those two, and it's a genuinely hard political question. And Washington is leaning in. The White House is convening a second, expanded ratepayer-protection summit, and Michigan's governor rolled out a data-center energy and water plan this week. This is the moment the AI buildout stops being a pure capex story and becomes a cost-of-living story. Power is turning into the political variable that gasoline prices always were — and in an election-conscious environment, no one wants to be the governor who let grandma's bill triple to subsidize a hyperscaler.
